title: Hungarian goulash
subtitle: Beef and paprika stew
excerpt: Beef chuck braised low and slow with sweet and smoked paprika, caraway, and tomatoes until the meat falls apart in a rich, brick-red sauce. The definitive comfort food of Central Europe. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Goulash (gulyás) is the national dish of Hungary, originating as a herdsman's stew of beef, onions, and paprika cooked in a cauldron over an open fire. The use of sweet Hungarian paprika as the defining spice is documented by George Lang in The Cuisine of Hungary (1971) and by Elisabeth Luard in European Peasant Cookery (1986). The sour cream garnish is customary in Hungary but optional.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to three months. Reheat gently on the hob.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. Improves on the second day.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make a day ahead — the sauce deepens overnight. Reheat gently and taste for seasoning before serving.

content.text: Goulash is one of those stews where the quality of the paprika makes the dish. Buy Hungarian sweet paprika if you can find it — it is more fragrant and less bitter than generic sweet paprika. The caraway seeds add a subtle anise note that is characteristic of the dish. Do not be tempted to add too many other spices; the flavour should be paprika-forward, with the beef and onions as the base.

items.prepNote: or two tablespoons of olive oil

content.text: Heat the oven to 160°C fan. Melt {{lard}} of lard in a heavy casserole over medium-high heat. Brown the beef cubes in batches for 3–4 minutes per batch until well coloured. Remove and set aside. Reduce the heat to medium. Add {{onion}} finely sliced onions to the pan and cook for 15 minutes until very soft and golden. Add {{garlic}} of garlic and {{caraway-seeds}} of caraway seeds; stir for 1 minute. Stir in {{paprika-sweet}} of sweet paprika and {{paprika-smoked}} of smoked paprika; cook for 30 seconds — do not let the paprika burn. Add {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée and stir for 1 minute. Return the beef to the pan. Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of hand-crushed tomatoes, {{stock-beef}} of beef stock, {{bay-leaves}} bay leaves, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Bring to a simmer, cover with a tight lid, and transfer to the oven. Braise for 1 hour 45 minutes to 2 hours until the beef is completely tender. Remove the bay leaves. Taste and adjust seasoning. Serve with egg noodles, mashed potato, or bread dumplings.

items.cause: Paprika added to too-hot fat and scorched.
items.fix: Add the paprika to the onions after removing the pan from high heat. Paprika burns in seconds at high temperature and turns acrid.

items.cause: Wrong cut (not enough collagen) or heat too low.
items.fix: Chuck or shin is correct. Lean braising cuts will not become tender regardless of cooking time. Add 30 minutes and check again.

items.cause: Too much stock, or lid not tight enough allowing too much evaporation.
items.fix: Remove the lid for the last 20 minutes and return to the oven to reduce. The sauce should coat the meat.

content.text: Goulash began as a herdsman's dish (gulyás means cowherd in Hungarian), made on the plains of the Puszta by cattle drovers who dried their cooked beef for the journey and reconstituted it with water and onions as needed. The paprika arrived in Hungary in the 16th century through the Ottoman occupation and became central to the national cooking within two centuries. The dish spread through Central Europe with the Habsburg Empire, appearing in Austria, the Czech lands, Slovakia, and southern Germany in various versions. The Hungarian original remains the most paprika-forward.
